text: The robotic procedure involves intracorporeal anastomosis after natural orifice specimen extraction for sigmoid resection. Four 8.5mm ports are placed in a straight line for access to the pelvic, left lower, and left upper quadrants. The surgeon extracts the intestine from the pelvis, marks the descending colon and sigmoid resection point, and lifts the sigmoid colon mesentery. A vessel sealer, a bipolar instrument, is introduced to divide the mesentery. Dissection continues into the pelvis, utilizing the fourth arm for retraction, mobilizing the sigmoid colon near the pelvic wall and left ureter. Inflammation and dense tissue are visible, completely freed and retracted from the pelvis. Further mobilization of the descending colon occurs along the line of Toldt, extending dissection to the left upper quadrant and splenic flexure.

The surgical team, including an attending and a fellow, operates the robotic arms to continue dividing the mesentery and distal colon using a vessel sealer bipolar energy device. The spleen is visible in the left upper quadrant as the surgeon approaches the splenic flexure, utilizing the port placement and docking for optimal access. Short bursts of bipolar energy are applied to coagulate and divide the bowel after preparation.

The intact left ureter is visible. A dilator is introduced, followed by ring forceps to extract the specimen trans-anally. The specimen is successfully extracted despite minor twisting difficulties. The proximal and distal ends are prepared for anastomosis, with a stay suture placed to hold them in position. Two 3-0 absorbable sutures are introduced, starting in the middle and running along the back wall.
The surgeon uses a needle driver and a bipolar grasper to perform a canal suture technique, starting the suture on the side to hold the anastomosis steady with the anvil for proper exposure.

The surgeon removes the dilator and continues the running suture technique on the anastomosis. The pelvis is filled with sterile water, and sigmoidoscopy is performed to check for leaks. Indocyanine green (ICG) is injected intravenously to assess the blood supply to the anastomosis, ensuring no ischemia. The area is suctioned, and sutures are divided to confirm there is no tension or twisting on the anastomosis.
